nativeRange.habitat: Aspidoras rochai is native to small, clear coastal streams and tributaries in the Brazilian state of Ceará. These are shallow, well-oxygenated upland drainages running over sandy or fine-gravel substrate, typically shaded by riparian vegetation and carpeted with leaf litter.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect delicate barbels
leaf litter (dried catappa or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dense planting at tank edges for security

diet.notes: Aspidoras rochai is a benthic forager that accepts a varied diet of sinking dried foods such as micro-pellets, catfish wafers, and crushed flake, as well as frozen or live small invertebrates including bloodworms, daphnia, micro-worms, and Tubifex. It should not be relied upon to survive on tank leftovers alone. Feeding sinking foods after lights out helps ensure each fish gets adequate nutrition. Offering dietary variety maintains good condition and supports breeding readiness.

goodWith: dwarf shrimp (neocaridina, caridina) in species-appropriate conditions
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving fish that out-compete at feeding

summary: Aspidoras rochai, also sold as the Guaramiranga Cory, is a tiny armored catfish endemic to coastal streams of Ceará, Brazil. A close relative of Corydoras, it stays small at 3.5 to 5 cm, is generally regarded as peaceful and gregarious, and thrives in groups of six or more in soft, slightly acidic water.
careNotes:
The Aspidoras genus is the sister-genus to Corydoras within the family Callichthyidae, and the two groups look nearly identical in body form and behavior. They are, however, taxonomically distinct: Aspidoras possess a different cranial bone structure — specifically, the frontoparietal fontanel (a small opening in the skull found in Corydoras) is absent or greatly reduced — and they are not assigned C-numbers the way Corydoras are. The 2024 revision of Corydoras taxonomy did not affect Aspidoras, which remains its own valid genus.

Aspidoras rochai stays small at 3.5 to 5 cm and is considerably less common in the aquarium hobby than most Corydoras. It originates from the coastal river drainages of Ceará state in north-eastern Brazil — relatively high-altitude streams that run clear, soft, and somewhat cooler than Amazonian lowland rivers. This explains its preference for the lower end of the tropical temperature range (20–26°C); it is best kept away from species that require warmer conditions of 28°C or above.

Aspidoras rochai is a social species and should be kept in groups of at least six, preferably more. Smaller groups tend to show stress-related behavioral suppression, with fish becoming withdrawn and less active. Fine sand substrate is essential to protect the delicate sensory barbels these catfish use for foraging; coarse gravel erodes the barbels over time. Like all members of Callichthyidae, this species is a facultative air-breather (meaning it can supplement gill respiration by gulping air at the surface) and will periodically dart upward to do so. A tight-fitting lid with some air gap is recommended. Excellent water quality with stable parameters and mature biological filtration is important; this species is best not introduced to a tank that has not fully cycled. Moderate, indirect flow is preferred over strong currents. A dimly lit setup with leaf litter and areas of shade closely reflects the natural habitat and tends to reduce stress.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows a similar pattern to Corydoras and has been achieved in home aquaria, though Aspidoras rochai is infrequently bred compared to popular Corydoras species. Begin by conditioning a group — ideally two or three males per female — on a varied diet of live and frozen foods over several weeks. Spawning is typically triggered by a significant water change of 30 to 50 percent using cooler, softer water, mimicking the onset of the rainy season; a concurrent drop in barometric pressure is thought to improve results. Eggs are adhesive and scattered across flat surfaces, plant leaves, or aquarium glass. Adults will consume eggs if given the opportunity, so eggs should be moved to a separate rearing container or protected with a mesh divider as soon as possible. Fry hatch in approximately three to five days and will accept micro-foods such as infusoria, micro-worms, and finely crushed flake from first feeding. Fry are delicate and sensitive to water quality; frequent small water changes in the rearing container are advisable.

primary.name: FishBase — Aspidoras rochai
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided maximum size (4.4 cm SL males/unsexed), temperature range (21–25°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (up to 14 dH), native distribution (Brazil, Ceará State coastal rivers), behavior (schooling, facultative air-breathing), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), IUCN status (Data Deficient, 2018), and diagnostic notes (body stripe pattern, preadipose plate variation). No photographs available on FishBase; only family-level illustration shown.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Aspidoras pauciradiatus (genus proxy)
additional.notes: No SeriouslyFish profile exists for A. rochai. A. pauciradiatus profile used as a close congener proxy for husbandry guidance: temp 20–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 36–215 ppm, minimum tank base 60×30 cm, fine sand substrate, omnivore diet (sinking foods plus live/frozen invertebrates), groups of 4–6 minimum, Corydoras-style breeding triggered by cool water change. Care parameters and breeding method applied to A. rochai with appropriate adjustments.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Corydoras/cory catfish care guide used as supplementary source for Callichthyidae husbandry norms applicable to Aspidoras: group size (6+), substrate (smooth sand or fine gravel), diet (sinking foods, live/frozen bloodworms, not primarily algae eaters), breeding triggers (cooler water change, conditioning on varied foods), compatibility (peaceful community). Temperature guidance for cooler-tolerant cories (72–82°F / 22–28°C) cross-referenced against FishBase data.

additional.name: FishBase — Aspidoras rochai species page (IUCN/ecology notes)
additional.notes: Secondary pass for ecological notes: benthopelagic habitat classification, facultative air-breathing confirmed, schooling behavior confirmed, IUCN Data Deficient status (assessed November 2018), low fishing vulnerability score. No depth-range data available; estimated 0–1 m based on stream habitat type. No image candidates identified — FishBase shows no photographs for this species and Wikimedia Commons search returned zero results.

fish.breedingNotes: Aspidoras rochai breeds in captivity using the same approach as Corydoras: a cool water change typically triggers spawning. Eggs are adhesive and scattered across surfaces; adults will eat them if not promptly separated. Fry are delicate and require micro-foods and pristine water quality. The species is infrequently bred in the hobby, largely owing to its limited availability.
